coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot unravels accumulation of triterpenoid in Evolvulus alsinoides hairy roots culture by stimulating growth, redox reactions and ANN machine learning model prediction of metabolic stress response

coredata.dc:description: Evolvulus alsinoides, a therapeutically valuable shrub can provide consistent supply of secondary metabolites (SM) with pharmaceutical significance. Nonetheless, because of its short life cycle, fresh plant material for research and medicinal diagnostics is severely scarce throughout the year. The effects of exogenous carbon quantum dot (CD) application on metabolic profiles, machine learning (ML) prediction of metabolic stress response, and SM yields in hairy root cultures of E. alsinoides were investigated and quantified. The range of the particle size distribution of the CDs was between 3 and 7 nm. The CDs EPR signal and spin trapping experiments demonstrated the formation of O2-•spin-adducts at (g = 2.0023). Carbon dot treatment increased the levels of hydrogen peroxide and malondialdehyde concentrations as well as increased antioxidant enzyme activity. CD treatments (6 μg mL-1) significantly enhanced the accumulation of squalene and stigmasterol (7 and 5-fold respectively). The multilayer perceptron (MLP) algorithm demonstrated remarkable prediction accuracy (MSE value = 1.99E-03 and R2 = 0.99939) in both the training and testing sets for modelling. Based on the prediction, the maximum oxidative stress index and enzymatic activities were highest in the medium supplemented with 10 μg mL-1 CDs. The outcome of this study indicated that, for the first time, using CD could serve as a novel elicitor for the production of valuable SM. MLP may also be used as a forward-thinking tool to optimize and predict SM with high pharmaceutical significance. This study would be a touchstone for understanding the use of ML and luminescent nanomaterials in the production and commercialization of important SM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Artificial neural network modelling the metabolic stress response; (A) Topology of the developed MLP network and (B) configuration structure of the ANN model (from Matlab software).

Establishment of hairy root cultures of Evolvulus alsinoides (L) leaves (A) Control leaves devoid of A. rhizogenes, (B) hairy root initiation on leaves after 5 days of infection. (C), (D) spontaneous lateral root branching within 8 and 12 days of incubation, (E) and (F) rapid division of the hairy root in solid basal MS media within 28 days.

Effect of various variables on the transformation efficiency of E. alsinoides leaves. (A) Growth of the bacterial (O.D.600), (B) days of co-cultivation time, (C) acetosyringone concentration, and (D) explant's age.
Characterization of carbon dot (A and C) high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), (B) SAED pattern, and (D) particle size distributions of the carbon dot.
Characterization of carbon dot (A) XRD plot, (B) Raman spectrum, (C) UV–visible, and (D) Fluorescence spectra of carbon dot.
Surface elements composition of the carbon dot (a) XPS survey, (b) S2p, (c) N1s, (d) C1s, (e) O1s of carbon dot, and (f) FTIR spectra of carbon dot.

Performance plot showing training, validation, and test data collected throughout the prediction of the optimal concentration. (A) Error histogram and (B) Performance status according to mean square error (MSE) vs epoch.

(a) Fresh weight, (b) dry weight, (c) total phenolic content, (d) total flavonoid content, (e) total triterpenoid content, (f) hydrogen peroxide content, (g) malondialdehyde content, (h) superoxide dismutase, (i) catalase, and (j) peroxidase enzyme activity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Evolvulus alsinoides, a therapeutically valuable shrub can provide consistent supply of secondary metabolites (SM) with pharmaceutical significance. Nonetheless, because of its short life cycle, fresh plant material for research and medicinal diagnostics is severely scarce throughout the year. The effects of exogenous carbon quantum dot (CD) application on metabolic profiles, machine learning (ML) prediction of metabolic stress response, and SM yields in hairy root cultures of E. alsinoides were investigated and quantified. The range of the particle size distribution of the CDs was between 3 and 7 nm. The CDs EPR signal and spin trapping experiments demonstrated the formation of O2-•spin-adducts at (g = 2.0023). Carbon dot treatment increased the levels of hydrogen peroxide and malondialdehyde concentrations as well as increased antioxidant enzyme activity. CD treatments (6 μg mL-1) significantly enhanced the accumulation of squalene and stigmasterol (7 and 5-fold respectively). The multilayer perceptron (MLP) algorithm demonstrated remarkable prediction accuracy (MSE value = 1.99E-03 and R2 = 0.99939) in both the training and testing sets for modelling. Based on the prediction, the maximum oxidative stress index and enzymatic activities were highest in the medium supplemented with 10 μg mL-1 CDs. The outcome of this study indicated that, for the first time, using CD could serve as a novel elicitor for the production of valuable SM. MLP may also be used as a forward-thinking tool to optimize and predict SM with high pharmaceutical significance. This study would be a touchstone for understanding the use of ML and luminescent nanomaterials in the production and commercialization of important SM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology, a rapidly growing field, has inspired substantial interest due to its myriad applications in plant biotechnology, biomedical engineering, energy resources, and material science. In plants, the application of these Nanocompounds has been reported by various researchers (Asgari-Targhi et al., 2018; Rivero-Montejo et al., 2021; Agri et al., 2022). These nanocompounds are easily absorbed by plant cells, potentially resulting in a consequential effect on their secondary metabolism (Zaytseva and Neumann, 2016). Therefore, they can be considered signal molecules in plant metabolism (Abideen et al., 2022).

Signal molecules are recognized for their ability to induce secondary metabolites (SM), and their use in cell cultures has become a successful approach. To this, a carbon nanomaterial-led revolution in secondary metabolites is unfolding to increase production efficiency and sustainability. For instance, Ghorbanpour and Hadian (2015) reported that multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) enhanced accumulation of SM (rosmarinic acid and caffeic acid), antioxidant content (H2O2) and enzyme activities (Polyphenol oxidase, L-Phenylalanine ammonia-lyase, and Peroxidase) in callus cultures of Satureja khuzestanica. Soraki et al. (2021) highlighted the beneficial effect of reduced graphene nanocomposite on enhancing ganoderic acid synthesis in Ganoderma lucidum. Recently, Peng et al. (2022) found that carbon dots stimulate Tetrastigma hemsleyanum growth and secondary metabolites by increasing gene expression for phenolic acid production. However, to the best of our knowledge, the effect of carbon dots on secondary metabolism in medicinal plants has yet to be completely explored.

Evolvulus alsinoides is a medicinal plant with therapeutic uses due to its myriad SM, such as terpenoids, phenolics, flavonoids, triterpenoids, and steroids (Kathirvel et al., 2021; Awere et al., 2024b). These compounds have antibacterial, antidiabetic, neuroprotective, anticancer, and anti-inflammatory effects. Some important secondary metabolites are squalene and stigmasterol. Stigmasterol is a bioactive substance that has been used as an anticancer against prostate, breast, gall bladder, cervical, lung, and colon cancer (Ahamed et al., 2022; Khan et al., 2022; Pandey et al., 2019). Furthermore, it has antimicrobial, neuroprotective, and anti-diabetic properties (Bakrim et al., 2022), while squalene is a secondary metabolite that is used as an emulsifier in adjuvants for influenza vaccinations, as well, it is an antioxidant and chemo-protective (Awere et al., 2024b). In addition, squalene has great moisturizing qualities and may profoundly hydrate the skin, making it a crucial component of many skin care treatments (Rameshkumar et al., 2018). These phytocompounds are well known to several pharmaceutical and nutraceutical companies, which are actively looking for and exploiting these compounds. The conventional method of harvesting E. alsinoides shoots from wild niches is disadvantageous due to indiscriminate harvesting, genetic degradation, and unreliable herbaceous material supply of secondary metabolites year-round. Therefore, in vitro culture techniques such as Agrobacterium-mediated transformation extremely offers sustainable sources of plant-derived natural products independent of climatic conditions and seasons, as well as resolving the aforementioned drawbacks through the use of elicitors (Niazian and Sabbatini, 2021).

Agrobacterium rhizogenes, a gram-negative soil bacterium naturally transfers a part of its DNA called T-DNA from the Ri plasmid (root-inducing) into the genome of the host plant. The Ri plasmid contains genes responsible for attachment, T-DNA processing, and plasmid transfer to the host plant's cell genome. TL T-DNA of A. rhizogenes contains Ri hairy root oncogenic locus genes, whereas TR T-DNA contains auxin and opine biosynthesis genes (Nilsson and Olsson, 1997). TL T-DNA takes part in hairy root induction despite being independently transported across the plant's genome (Chandra, 2012). The involvement of root locus genes in hairy root formation has been investigated in agropine-type strains, with eighteen open reading frames (ORF) have been discovered in agropine-type Ri T-DNA (Cardarelli et al., 1987; Veena and Taylor, 2007). The ORFs correspond to root locus genes, also known as rol genes (A, B, C, and D). The transfer of these genes results in the induction and proliferation of hairy roots at the point of infection in plants (Nilsson and Olsson, 1997; Awere et al., 2024a).

Regression-based models cannot explain the extremely nonlinear and intricate interaction (Niazian et al., 2018). Plant scientists have recently been intrigued by applying machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI) models to solve this challenge. The use of different artificial neural network (ANN) modelling and ML in plant science is a comparatively state-of-the-art field applied in optimization and prediction of variables in systems of living organisms (biological systems) (Hesami et al., 2021; Aasim et al., 2022). The use of ANN and machine learning has recently been reported in tissue and in vitro cultures, for instance, for the optimization and prediction of in vitro micropropagation (Sadat-Hosseini et al., 2022), cell culture (García-Pérez et al., 2020), elicitation in calli cultures (Salehi et al., 2021), germination (Hesami et al., 2021), and somatic embryogenesis (Hesami et al., 2019, 2020). The performance of any model may vary depending on the variables (inputs) used and the experimental design, therefore, selecting an appropriate model is based on various factors for accurate and precise prediction of results (Hesami et al., 2021; Aasim et al., 2022).

To the best of our knowledge, 1) there has been no previous research reported for Agrobacterium transformation in E. alsinoides; 2) the application of carbon dot as a signal molecule on hairy root cultures of medicinal plants; and 3) biochemical analysis, machine learning, and artificial neural network prediction of the phytochemicals in vitro cultures of E. alsinoides. Therefore, this study attempted to evaluate the potential impact of carbon dots at varied concentrations on the hairy root growth of E. alsinoides. Secondary metabolite responses and HPTLC quantification of squalene and stigmasterol in the hairy root extracts were also examined.

Reduced glutathione, Formamide, Acetosyringone, Cefotaxime, Yeast extract, Sodium chloride (NaCl), Peptone, Calcium chloride (CaCl2 2H2O), Ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3), Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4 7H2O), Potassium monophosphate (KH2PO4), Pyridoxine HCL, Cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2 6H2O), Potassium nitrate (KNO3), Ferrous sulfate (FeSO4 7H2O), Boric acid (H3BO3), Thiamine HCL, Manganese sulfate tetrahydrate (MnSO4 4H2O), Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4 2H2O), Glacial acetic acid, Nicotinic Acid, Potassium iodide (KI), Glycine, Ethylenediaminetetraacetic Acid, methanol, Agar, Sucrose, Anisaldehyde, Zinc sulfate (ZnSO4·7H2O), Disodium Salt, Dihydrate (Na2 EDTA. 2H2O), Myo-Inositol, Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4 5H2O), and sulfuric acid.

The present investigation used 4 wild-type strains of A. rhizogenes (A4, 2364, R1000, and 532) that had been maintained in our laboratory (Shilpha et al., 2015). 2364 and 532 were initially obtained from Microbial Type Culture Collection (MTCC), while A4 and R1000 were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). The stains were cultured in 3 mL of YEP (Yeast Extract Peptone) broth overnight at 27 °C in a refrigerated incubator shaker (Scigenics Biotech Orbitek, 220 rpm). Following incubation, 200 μl of the strains were subcultured into fresh YEP broth of similar volume and incubated using the same conditions. After 12 h, the strains were streaked onto a solid YEP medium and incubated overnight at 27 °C. A single colony of the strains was cultured in 30 mL of YEP broth and incubated for 6–18 h (to obtain different O.D. values) at 27 °C in a refrigerated incubator shaker (220 rpm). Bacterial cells were harvested using a centrifuge (ESCO Versati; TCR - 1500–8) in log phase, with O.D.600 = 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0. Centrifugation of the bacterial suspension was performed at 4 °C, 8000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting pellet was resuspended in 30 mL of liquid MS basal medium (Shilpha et al., 2015).

For optimum transformation efficiency, various parameters such as OD values, age of the explant, co-cultivation time and acetosyringone concentration were optimized (Fig. 3A–D). In vitro-proliferating leaves of E. alsinoides (45, 70, and 90 days old) were excised from the cultures maintained in our laboratory. The leaves were used as an explant for transformation studies. Different acetosyringone concentrations (100, 200, and 300 mM) were added to the bacterial suspension and considered for infection medium. Leaves were pricked using an autoclaved pricking needle and further immersed in the bacterial and acetosyringone suspension for 5 min with gentle shaking. The leaves were then co-cultivated at 24 ± 2 °C for a period between 0 and 10 days in the dark. After every period of co-cultivation, the leaves were rinsed in liquid basal MS medium prepended with 500 mgL−1 of cefotaxime. The leaves were blotted using autoclaved tissue papers and inoculated on basal solid MS medium prepended with cefotaxime (500 mg L−1) for hairy roots induction. Pricked explants immersed in MS media devoid of A. rhizogenes were used as a control.

A PCR was used to validate the presence of the Ri TDNA genes for rolA in transformed roots. Isolation of genomic DNA from random transformed root clones was performed using the CTAB procedure with some modifications (HiPurA kit (HiMEDIA, Mumbai, India) (Doyle and Doyle, 1987). The DNA of the non-transformed roots was used as a negative control, while positive controls for rolA gene amplification included plasmid DNA from A. rhizogenes and genomic DNA from hairy roots. The primers used to amplify the rolA gene fragment were forward (5′-ACGGTGAGTGTGGTTGTAGG-3′) and reverse (5′-GCCACGTGCGTATTAATCCC-3′). The rolA gene is the 10th of the 18 open reading frames of the TL-DNA. Its size ranges from 279 to 423 bp in length. It also contains the promotor, 5′- and 3′-UTR, and polyadenylation sites (Veremeichik et al., 2023). The rolA gene promoter contains three domains: A, B, and C. The A domain regulates the expression of the rolA gene (Guivarc'h et al., 1996). The B domain encompasses a TATA box (Xue et al., 2008). The C domain of the non-coding strand of the rolA gene includes TGACG sequences motifs from the 35S promotor of cauliflower mosaic virus (Veremeichik et al., 2023). The PCR reaction volume was 19.5 μL containing 2 μL Taq buffer, 0.125 μL dNTPs, 0.5 M primer, 0.2 μL of DNA polymerase (Taq), 1 μL of template DNA, and 15.175 Milli Q water. The PCR amplification conditions of the rolA gene process were performed for 5 min (94 °C) for initial denaturation and 1 min (94 °C) for denaturation. The annealing temperature was set for 45 s (at 55 °C), 72 °C for 45 s, and 72 °C for 5 min for extension and final extension, respectively. Prior to being dispensed into the boat, ethidium bromide (EtBr) (0.5 μg/mL) was added to 1 × TAE buffer containing 1.2% agarose gel. 2 μL of 6 × DNA loading dye (Thermo Scientific) was mixed with the amplified products and resolved onto the solidified gel. Visualization was done using the Gel DocXR + documentation system (Gel DocXR+, BIO-RAD, QuarryBay, Hong Kong).

Single-step microwave-assisted carbonization was used for the synthesis of carbon dots. 5 g of reduced glutathione was dispersed in formamide (50 mL). The mixture was sonicated for 30 min until it formed a commensurate solution. The homogeneous solution was then placed in a household microwave oven (700 W) for 2 min until a dark green solution was formed. The commensurate solution was further sonicated for 1 h, after which the dark green solution was placed in a dialysis tube for 10 days. The resulting solution was lyophilized to form a dark green powder (Wang et al., 2021).

A household microwave (IFB, Model: 30SC4) was used for the synthesis of carbon dot. Lyophilizer (IG-LZ10T; India) was used for dying the carbon dot solution to obtain solids. An ultrasonic vibrator (VCX 750, Newtown USA) was used for the dispersion of the solid into the liquid. Surface composition measurements were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI Versa Probe III; Japan) and fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) (Thermo fisher; USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL Service pvt Ltd: 2100 plus; Japan) imaging was performed to describe the morphology of the carbon dot. XRD (Bruker D8 Advance; USA) and Micro-Laser Raman spectroscopy (Seiki, Japan) were used to check the crystallinity of the carbon dot. HPTLC (CAMAG make HPTLC; Switzerland) was used for the quantification of secondary metabolites. EPR spectroscopy (EMX Plus X Band; BRUKER BIOSPIN, Germany) was performed to identify the presence of unpaired electrons. UV–VIS (Evolution 201; Thermo Fisher) spectroscopy and Jasco Spectrofluorometer (FP 8300; Japan) spectroscopy were used for recording the absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra, respectively, of the carbon dot.

The carbon nanomaterial elicitor was dispersed in autoclaved distilled water, and filter sterilized. Concentrations of 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 μg mL−1 of the carbon nanomaterial were prepended into the liquid MS medium. The initial weights of hairy roots were recorded, after which they were subcultured in a conical (250 mL) flask containing 100 mL of modified MS liquid medium (MS medium + CDs) under constant shaking at 180 rpm at 26 °C in an incubator. The data on biomass accumulation was recorded after 28 days of culture incubation. All the treatments were carried out in triplicate.

Evolvulus alsinoides hairy roots were washed using distilled water to eliminate all media components. The hairy roots and fresh leaves from wild plants were shriveled in a hot air oven at 60 °C. The dry biomass was powdered using a grinder, resulting in a finer powder. The resulting powder (2 g) was extracted in a Soxhlet apparatus with 200 mL of methanol at 64.7 °C for for 20 cycles. After extraction, a vacuum evaporator was used for drying the extract. Desired concentration was obtained by adding methanol to the resultant solid deposits. The resulting extract from each of the treatments was analyzed independently for the qualitative and quantitative assessment of phytochemicals. The extract was later exposed to HPTLC quantification of squalene and stigmasterol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HPTLC analysis of the secondary metabolites
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of 1 mg/mL of stigmasterol and squalene was prepared separately. Aliquots of 0.5 μg–3 μg solution were prepared from the stock. Different concentrations (0.5–3 μg/spot) of squalene and stigmasterol were spotted on the TLC plate. Further, 10 μL of the plant extract solution were spotted onto the chromatographic plates. The volume of stigmasterol and squalene present in the extracts was estimated by means of the standard calibration plot. Mobile solvents toluene (8.9): acetone (0.9): acetic acid (0.2) (v/v/v) were used for the proper separation of squalene and stigmasterol. Derivatization of the TLC plates was done using an anisaldehyde-sulfuric (AS) reagent. The reagent (AS) was prepared by mixing 0.5 mL of anisaldehyde with 10 mL of glacial acetic acid (GAA). Precooled methanol (85 mL) and 5 mL of chilled concentrated H2SO4 (sulfuric acid) (Sigma-Aldrich, Munich, Germany) were added to the solution. All the chemicals utilized for the AS reagent formulation were ice-cold. The plates were sprayed with the AS reagent. After spraying, the plates were blown by a hair dryer to keep them dry. The plate was further heated using an oven at 60 °C for 2 min. The RF values of the standards squalene and stigmasterol were matched with bands of the plant extracts for identification and quantification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total phenolics, triterpenoid and flavonoids content
ce:section.ce:para: Total phenolics in the hairy root extracts was estimated by the protocol described by Slinkard and Singleton (1977), with slight modifications. Distilled water (2 mL) was added to 100 μl of the hairy root extract in 48-well plates. 1 mL of Foline-Ciocalteu reagent (FC) was added to the mixture and incubated for 3 min, after which 1 mL of Na2CO3 (20% w/v) solution was incorporated into the mixture. The reaction solutions were continuously shaken in the dark for 45 min. The absorbance measurements were read at 725 nm. Total phenolics were expressed as mg of gallic acid equivalent per g of extract dry weight. Total triterpenoid composition was executed via the technique established by Wei et al. (2015). Hairy root extracts (100 μL) were mixed with 150 μL of vanillin acetic acid (5% w/v) solution. 500 μL of sulfuric acid was added into the sample solution that already contained plant extract and vanillin acetic acid. The solution was incubated for 15 min at 60 °C. Following incubation period, the sample was cooled in an ice water bath for 20 min 200 μL of the samples were loaded into 48-well plates, and 800 μl of acetic acid was further added to the solution. Absorbance was determined at 570 nm.

The aluminium chloride assay reported by Zhishen et al. (1999) was performed for the quantification of flavonoid composition in the hairy root extracts. In light of this, 300 μL of NaNO2 (5%) was dispersed in 4 mL of water in a tube, and the extract (1 mL) was further added into the test tube. The solution was incubated for 5 min. Following the incubation period, the solution was supplemented with 10% AlCl3 (300 μL), 2 mL of 1 M NaOH solution, and 10 mL of water, and it was agitated for 30 min in the dark. Following incubation, absorbance was determined at 510 nm. Flavonoid compositions were expressed as milligram of quercetin equivalents per gram of extract dry biomass. All the experiments were performed in triplicate. All the absorbance measurements were performed using a SpectraMax® M3 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of H2O2 and MDA contents
ce:section.ce:para: The technique of Velikova et al. (2000) was used to ascertain the H2O2 concentration in the extract. 600 mg of fresh hairy root samples were crushed in 0.1% w/v trichloroacetic acid (6 mL) in ice. The crushed mixture was then centrifuged for 20 min at 10,000 rpm. Following centrifugation, the supernatant (0.5 mL) was added to a solution containing 0.5 mL of potassium phosphate (K3PO4) buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) and 1 M potassium iodide (1 mL). The absorbance measurements were recorded at 390 nm. Malondialdehyde (MDA) production was quantified via thiobarbituric acid (TBA) reaction to determine the lipid peroxidation level. MDA content was expressed using the methods of Heath and Packer (1968). To calculate the contents of MDA, the extinction coefficient (155 mM−1 cm−1) was applied for its quantity estimation; the findings were expressed as μM g−1 FW. All the experiments were conducted in triplicate.

The hairy root samples (500 mg) were pulverized in 50 mM sodium phosphate buffer (1.5 mL, pH 7.8) at 4 °C with 0.1 mM EDTA integrated together. The centrifugation settings were 12,000 rpm for 40 min at 4 °C. Following centrifugation, the resulting supernatant was collected in a clean tube and utilized for the subsequent enzyme tests.

With a few minor adjustments, the procedure outlined by Abeles and Biles (1991) was used for the determination of the total POD activity. Reaction mixtures with a final volume of 1 mL were used to quantify the peroxidase activity. 100 μl of the sample was incubated in 0.05 M acetate buffer (pH 5) with guaiacol (1 mM) and H2O2 (1 mM). The absorbance was measured at 470 nm every 15 s for 2 min. POD activity was expressed as μkat. mg−1 protein.

The SOD's activity was measured using a protocol outlined by Beauchamp and Fridovich (1973). A total of 2 mL of reaction volume mixture was used: 2 μM riboflavin, enzyme extract (5 μL), 13 mM methionine, 75 μM nitrobluetetrazolium, and 1 mL 50 mM phosphate buffer pH 7.8. The enzyme quantity required to achieve an inhibition rate of 50% of NBT degradation at 560 nm was determined as the total SOD enzyme activity. The SOD activity was reported as a U mg−1 protein. The experiment was performed in triplicate.

Catalase activity was quantified spectrophotometrically following the methods of Aebi (1983). Before performing the experiment, the enzyme extract was diluted 1:14 times with buffer. A 1.5 mL of 30 mM H2O2 and 1.5 mL of enzyme extract were mixed. The degradation of H2O2 was observed spectrophotometrically upon a decline in absorbance at 240 nm. CAT was expressed as μkat. mg−1 protein.

An ANN model was developed for the prediction of 10 different variables using concentration and initial weight of the hairy root as the input. The fresh weight, dry weight, total phenolics, triterpenoid, and flavonoids content, malondialdehyde, hydrogen peroxide content, catalase, superoxide dismutase, and peroxidase enzyme activity values were estimated in the output layer of the designed multilayer perceptron (MLP) network model. The architecture of the designed MLP network is represented in Fig. 1 A. The model was trained using 75% of the input data, while 25% was utilized for testing. The Bayesian Regularization algorithm was preferred as the training algorithm for the MLP network model. The array of neurons to be applied in the hidden layer of ANN models was determined by analyzing models with different numbers of neurons and a model with 10 neurons in the hidden layer was determined. Fig. 1 B shows the configuration structure of the ANN model. To evaluate the efficiency of the designed ANN model, performance parameters that are often utilized in the existing literature were ascertained. Calculation of the performance parameters i.e. mean squared error (MSE), coefficient of correlation (R) and margin of deviation (MoD) values are represented by the following formulas (Çolak et al., 2023)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electro paramagnetic resonance study of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The method used for the EPR studies was determined according to methods described by Merunka and Peric (2019). The carbon dot was dissolved in water at room temperature. The carbon dot solution was added into 5-μL capillaries (radius = 150 μm) and encased at the bottom, leaving the upper end exposed, just before the EPR experiments. A Varian E−109 X-band spectrometer that had been equipped with a Bruker microwave bridge and a Bruker high-Q cavity was used to record EPR spectra. A thermocouple and an Omega temperature indicator were used to determine the sample temperature that was maintained at a constant ±0.2 K under the guidance of a Bruker variable temperature unit. The measurement was done in increments of 5 K throughout a range of temperatures. The parameters used to collect the EPR spectra were 20 s sweep duration, 0.5 mW microwave frequency, 16 ms time constant, 0.1 G modulated amplitude, and 50 G sweep width.

Agrobacterium rhizogenes transformation of E. alsinoides were conducted in three replicates with 30 explants in each experimental treatment. The data on optimum transformation efficiency was determined by calculating the percentages. A two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed using the Statistical Analysis System (SAS) software to evaluate if there was significant variation in the average H2O2 and MDA contents, enzyme assays, fresh weight, dry weight, total triterpenoid, phenolic and flavonoid content with respect to the CDs supplemented into the medium. Least significance difference (LSD) was used to determine the difference between the means with p < 0.0001 considered significant. A molecular analysis assessment was conducted using the rolA primer. AI and ML were used for predicting phytochemical, antioxidant and enzyme analysis.

Evolvulus alsinoides is a tropical plant that sprouts during the first monsoon rainfall. The Indian traditional medical systems have attributed considerable therapeutic value to E. alsinoides (Mettupalayam et al., 2020), which highlights the plant's potential to be used for the production of plant-based pharmaceuticals. The consistent generation of these medicinally significant phytochemicals necessitates a consistent source of robust plant material. In vitro tissue culture techniques provide the benefit of developing clones for fast growth by reducing seed reliance and increasing secondary metabolite content.

Transformation efficiency is influenced by different variables such as acetosyringone concentration, co-cultivation time, bacterial strain, optical density (O.D.), type of the explant, and explant age (Moehninsi and Navarre, 2018; Qin et al., 2021). Four A. rhizogenes strains were used during the infection. The needle-pricked parts on the leaves turned to brown spots. Hairy roots emerged from the brown spots on the leaves and were first observed after five days of incubation in the dark (Fig. 2 B). This was attributed to the high auxin and sucrose enrichment in phloem cells inside the leaves, which stimulates the expression of rol genes and the production of roots (Nilsson and Olsson, 1997).

Similarly, earlier reports of higher transformation efficiency from leaf explants have been reported in Bacopa monnieri (Bansal et al., 2014). The non-infected control explants showed no signs of a predisposition to develop roots (Fig. 2A), the hairy roots were able to emerge both at the petiole region and the abaxial surface of leaf explants (Fig. 2B). Our findings demonstrated that the rate of transformation was dependent on the optical density and strain of A. rhizogenes used. MTCC 532 and A4 showed a higher transformation rate (80%) at 0.6 O.D.600, while the ATCC 2364 and R1000 strains required an optical density of 0.8 and 1.0, respectively (Fig. 3 A). The varying effects of bacterial growth have recently also been reported (Moehninsi and Navarre, 2018; Qin et al., 2021). Four days of cultivation time was recorded to be optimally efficient for transformation in 532, 2364, and A4, while R1000 was optimally efficiently transformed on the 8th day (Fig. 3B). Similarly, an optimal cultivation time of 4 days has been reported in Semecarpus anacardium (Panda et al., 2017). Longer co-cultivation time led to necrosis of the leaf, leading to death. Incorporation of acetosyringone into the co-cultivation medium improved the transformation efficiency. Our findings demonstrated that the addition of 200 mM acetosyringone was found to be efficient in enhancing the transformation rate (Fig. 3C). Similarly, earlier reports of a higher transformation rate upon addition of 200 mM acetosyringone have also been reported in Solanum trilobatum (Shilpha et al., 2015). Agrobacterium-mediated transformation efficiency is also greatly influenced by the physiological status of the tissue and the maturity of the explant. Our results revealed that a 45-day-old explant was efficient for an optimal transformation rate in E. alsinoides (Fig. 3D). On the contrary, older leaves turned necrotic, thus reducing transformation efficiency. Variation of the explant's age for optimal transformation rate has been reported earlier in Echinacea angustifolia, Glycine max (Khalili et al., 2014; Niazian et al., 2023) respectively.

The HRTEM provided evidence that the synthesized nanomaterial was a carbon quantum dot (Fig. 4 A). The SAED pattern revealed broad circular rings, which confirmed the amorphous nature of the prepared carbon dot (Fig. 4B). The diameters of the carbon dots are centered at 3 − 7.5 nm (Fig. 4D). The HRTEM image showed a well-resolved fringe of the lattice with an interplanar d-spacing of 0.201 nm, similar to the (002) diffraction facets of the graphitic carbon (Fig. 4C) (Nie et al., 2014). The XRD spectra peak at 26.93° and 44.20° (Fig. 5 A) corresponds to the crystal planes 002 and 101, which are associated with the presence of an amorphous carbonaceous compound. The XRD spectrum verifies the non-crystalline graphite structure of the porous carbons (Wang et al., 2021). The Raman spectrum displayed 2 peaks at 1563 and 1367 cm−1 that were associated with the bands G and D, respectively, and corresponded to the sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons, respectively (Fig. 5B). The ratio of intensity of the D and G peaks (ID/IG) was 0.87, suggesting partial defects in carbon-based materials (Y. Hu et al., 2014). The UV absorption spectrum of the carbon dot exhibits three absorption peaks at 258 nm, 322 nm, and 419 nm. The peaks are typically ascribed to the π → π∗, π → π∗, and n → π∗ transitions of the aromatic C=C bond and the π aromatic system that possesses the C=N, C=O, and C=S bonds, respectively (Fig. 5C) (Wang et al., 2021). The carbon dot exhibited an excitation-independent fluorescence emission with an excitation wavelength of 448 nm (Fig. 5D). In the.

The XPS analysis was further employed to determine the binding energy, oxidation states, and surface elements of the carbon dot. The XPS survey scan of the nanomaterial confirmed the presence of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p elements (Fig. 6 a). The high-resolution spectra were also thought to be crucial for broader comprehension. The deconvoluted S2p spectrum was fitted to two peaks at binding energies of 162.08 eV S 2p 3/2 C-S and 163.38 eV S 2p ½ (Fig. 6b). The deconvoluted N1s spectrum reveals three distinct peaks at 399.5 eV (pyridinic Ns), 398.7 eV (quaternary Ns), and 400.3 eV (pyrrolinic Ns) (Fig. 6c). The deconvoluted C1s region revealed two distinctive peaks at 284.7 and 287.5 eV, corresponding to C-C and C=O, respectively (Fig. 6d). The deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum was fitted into two peaks at binding energies of 531.7 and 533.0 eV, which represent C=O and C-OH, revealing the formation of oxygen functional groups on the surfaces of the carbon dot (Fig. 6e). (Nie et al., 2014; Y. Hu et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2021). In the FT-IR analysis of the carbon dots (Fig. 6f), the absorption band at 3444 cm−1 is attributed to the O-H stretching vibrations, demonstrating the existence of hydrophilic hydroxyl. The peaks at 3319 and 2889 cm−1 belong to the N-H stretching vibrations. The peak at 1677 and 2887 cm−1 is attributed to the C=N stretching vibrations, indicating an abundance of nitrogen in the synthesized nanomaterial. The peaks at 1389 and 1315 cm−1 are ascribed to C-H and S=O bending and stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption peak at 1055 cm−1 is imputed to S=O and C-O stretching vibrations (Nie et al., 2014; Y. Hu et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2021). These FT-IR bands are corroborated by XPS analysis, which shows that the carbon dots consist of C, N, O, and S atoms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of carbon dot on hairy root biomass production
ce:section.ce:para: Adding CDs to the MS medium increased the fresh and dry weights of the hairy roots compared to the standard medium (control) (r = 0.992483) (Table 1 ). There was a significant difference (p < 0.05) in the mean biomass values across different concentrations of carbon dot. 6 μg mL−1 of CD was significantly different from other concentrations, with the highest mean biomass (1.7756), while the control (devoid of elicitor) had the lowest mean of 0.9604. The measured values for fresh weight across different concentrations also showed significant variation. Relative to the control, high concentrations of CDs (8 and 10 μg mL−1) suppressed the growth of E. alsinoides hairy roots and biomass accumulation, which were consistent with the high ROS content observed under such treatments. Our results are consistent with previous studies on the effects of ROS production on biomass accumulation, for example, Ghorbanpour and Hadian (2015) reported that the high concentration of multi-walled carbon nanotubes reduced the biomass accumulation in S. khuzestanica callus culture.

Table 1 illustrates the total phytochemical content observed in E. alsinoides hairy roots following exposure to various CDs concentrations. A strong correlation (r = 0.99939) existed between TPC, TTC, TFC and carbon dot concentration. There was a significant difference (p < 0.05) in the TPC accumulation across different concentrations of CDs. Ten μg mL−1 of CDs had the highest production of TPC (30.0972) compared to the control (15.0359) hairy roots. Similarly, in TFC and TTC, 6 μg mL−1 of CDs was significantly different compared to the control hairy roots with the highest mean of 8.2871 and 41.0272 respectively, while the control had the lowest mean TFC and TTC (2.6130 and 30.1926) accumulations in hairy roots respectively. Two μg mL−1 of CDs did not significantly enhance the total phytochemical content of the hairy roots in comparison to the control.

Evidence from the literature suggests that oxidative stress caused by nanomaterials can increase synthesis of phenolic, triterpenoid, and flavonoid molecules (Tang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2022). Following our findings, lowest and maximum concentrations of carbon dot exhibited similar stimulatory results in the accumulation trends of triterpenoid, phenolic, and flavonoid molecules in E. alsinoides hairy roots. In our study, all the treatment resulted in a small but non-significant rise in H2O2 concentration compared to control samples. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) can cause metabolic shifts under stress conditions. However, in higher concentrations, ROS can be hazardous and destroy cells (Garg and Manchanda, 2009). Rahmani et al. (2020) established that ROS plays a vital role in signalling molecules for SM synthesis in plants exposed to nanomaterials. The subjection of the hairy roots to carbon dot treatment appeared to increase phenolic content in E. alsinoides. In plants, phenolic compounds function as potent antioxidants and free radical scavengers as a result of their redox attributes (Grace-Lynn et al., 2012; Thatoi et al., 2014). In the light of this research outcome, we can speculate that the increased accumulation of total content phenolics in response to higher concentrations of carbon dot could be attributed to the antioxidant activities of these SM against ROS at higher concentrations. Our results is consistent with that of Peng et al. (2022) who reported that CD treatment in T. hemsleyanum increased the accumulation of TPC while it greatly reduced the TFC. Following treatment of T. hemsleyanum with CDs, there was a higher accumulation of secondary metabolites associated with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties (syringin, neochlorogenic acid, homogentisic acid, 1-and 2-caffeoylquinic acid) while there was low accumulation of secondary metabolites linked to anti-tumor action, notably apigenin, quercetin, and kaempferol glycoside derivatives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of carbon dot on H2O2, MDA content and enzymatic activities
ce:section.ce:para: The increase of H2O2 in plants is generally referred to as the “oxidative burst,” and it signals antioxidant defence mechanisms in response to various forms of stress (Qiu et al., 2021). Several researchers have indicated that plants generally respond to both metal and carbon-based nanomaterials by producing oxidative stress (Marslin et al., 2017; Rahmani et al., 2020; Suman et al., 2021). Increased production of ROS serves as a sign of cellular damage in oxidative stress (Sharma et al., 2012). The MDA and H2O2 generation showed significant difference between the treated hairy roots and the control. Exposure of hairy roots to high concentrations of CDs (10 μg mL−1) had a substantial effect on the MDA (1.3433) and H2O2 (1.9039) contents in the hairy roots of E. alsinoides when compared to control 0.1542 and 0.3542 respectively (Table 2 ). There was a positive correlation between the MDA and H2O2 contents in the hairy root exposed to carbon dot. Furthermore, the evoked hairy roots displayed a browning colour in comparison to the control roots, indicating that oxidative stress following elicitation, which is in agreement with the results previously reported by Chong et al. (2005; Jiao et al. (2018). Chong et al. (2005) observed a comparable increase in H2O2 level in Morinda elliptica cell cultures upon addition of the nanomaterial. Our results are also consistent with those of Rahmani et al. (2020) who observed that exposure of Salvia verticillata leaves to higher concentrations of CNTs significantly enhanced MDA and H2O2 generation by 2.8 and 1.8 fold higher than those seen in the control leaves respectively.

Furthermore, there was significant difference in the hairy root treated with CDs compared to the control. The activities of antioxidant enzymes in treated hairy roots increased significantly as the concentration of carbon dot increased (Table 2). Application of a 10 μg mL−1 carbon dot substantially increased SOD, and POD activity in hairy roots (8.5558, and 3.0969 respectively) compared to control. Moreover, correlations were found between the POD and SOD activities of the hairy roots. SOD is widely known for acting as a crucial initial enzyme in plants' antioxidant defence mechanism against stress. Whenever SOD is up-regulated in plant's cells, it is accompanied by increased activity of other radical scavenging enzymes which also function as effective modules of defensive systems against oxidative bursts (Sharma et al., 2012; Rahmani et al., 2020). Our findings demonstrate that even at lower concentrations of carbon dots (2 μg mL−1), antioxidant enzymes effectively removed H2O2 and protected hairy root cells from harm. Our results are comparable to those of other researchers who have used carbon materials in plant growth and physiology. For instance, Fan et al. (2018) demonstrated that 50 mg L−1 of MWCNTs in the culture medium were unable to cause more oxidative stress in Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings. However, higher concentrations of the carbon nanoparticle were effective in increasing cellular injuries and oxidative damages associated with increased H2O2 generation, elevated MDA levels, and antioxidant enzyme activity.

Our results are also consistent with those of Ghorbanpour and Hadian (2015), who also recounted that the PPO and POD activities and H2O2 accumulation were dependent on the concentration of the nanomaterial in Satureja khuzestanica calli extracts that were cultured in media supplemented with varying concentrations of MWCNTs. Similarly, other researchers have reported the oxidative stress induced in a dose-dependent way by various carbon-based nanomaterials such as SWCNT in Hyoscyamus niger (Hatami et al., 2017). Our research has shown that, unlike previous carbon nanomaterials, our nanomaterial triggered the peroxidation of lipids and the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the hairy roots of E. alsinoides at a lower concentration. As one of the powerful defence strategies, the hairy roots of E. alsinoides seemed to have activated their antioxidant defence system to safeguard the cells from stress (oxidative damage) due to exposure of carbon dot. After being exposed to high concentrations of oxidative stressors, plant cells may experience necrosis, apoptosis, or structural damage as a result of the excessive generation of H2O2 (Sharma et al., 2019).

Application of distinct algorithms (ML) provides a more facile and accurate approach to recognizing and diagnosing intricate data sets acquired from tissue culture studies (Jafari and Daneshvar, 2023). These technologies overcome discrepancies related to different responses by examining intricate trends and utilizing algorithms models to predict the most effective variables for optimum outcomes (Niazian and Niedbała, 2020). In view to verify the training phase of the developed ANN models, the data obtained from the training phase was examined. When the performance graph shown (Fig. 7 A) is examined, it is observed that the MSE values are low at the start of the training phase, decrease with the following stages, and the training phase of the model ends when the ideal value was reached. In the histogram given in Fig. 7B, it was observed that the errors obtained from the training phase are very low. These results confirm that the training phase of the designed ANN model was completed accurately.

Carbon nanomaterials usually attach themselves to the cell's surface and form filamentous structures in clusters (Agarwal et al., 2022). They also penetrate plant cell walls and alter metabolic processes through hydrogen bonding between the nanomaterial and cell wall constituents or components (for instance, groups containing nitrogen) (Hu et al., 2014). There was a significant difference in the accumulation of biomass from E. alsinoides in response to CDs treatment (Table 2). The MS medium devoid of the CDs had the lowest (0.9604 g) (Fig. 12 A) while 6 μg mL-1 of CDs was very effective for high biomass accumulation (1.7756 g) (Fig. 12B). The potential of these CDs to stimulate hairy root biomass accumulation could be due to the charge, size and functional groups present on its surface, which may have improved plant growth following CD treatment (Villagarcia et al., 2012). Higher concentrations of carbon dot had a deleterious impact on hairy root cell viability and growth ratio (Fig. 12C), they suppressed the growth of hairy roots and biomass accretion of E. alsinoides hairy roots. The solid MS media was not effective for increased biomass accumulation (Fig. 12D). Carbon-based nanomaterials have been reported to exhibit a deleterious influence on plant cells due to their shape and size, solubility, chemical composition, aggregation, and surface structure (Zhai et al., 2015) and excessive formation of ROS in a concentration-dependent manner (Mathur et al., 2023), resulting in structural damage in cells and organelles and eventual death. The findings of our present research are also consistent with those of (Zoufan et al., 2020), who reported that nanomaterials had a differential impact based on species of the plant and that the deleterious effect is dependent on concentration and time of treatments. The biomass growth rate is proportional to the amount of nanomaterials applied because of their unique attributes, such as surface chemistry, attachment type, and biocompatibility (Ghorbanpour and Hadian, 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of O2 − radical formation and elicitation
ce:section.ce:para: Unpaired electron spins in materials are investigated by electroparamagnetic spectroscopy to reveal structural details (Davies, 2016). Activated oxygen radicals (O2 •−) are formed when free electrons interact with O2 molecules (Elstner, 1982). We employed EPR technology to evaluate the potential of carbon nanomaterials to activate O2, and the test was conducted in darkness at room temperature. The EPR spectra of the CDs nanomaterials showed a distinct signal at g = 2.0023 caused by the generation of O2 •− (Fig. 13 A) (Pan et al., 2021). This study clearly showed that the CDs irrespective of their surface chemistry or structural characteristics may perform O2 activation at normal temperatures. Fig. 13B displays the postulated approach for oxygen activation using carbon-dot micropores shackles at room temperature. The process involves high oxygen adsorption in ultramicropores and the transfer of electrons from the carbon matrix, creating the O2 •− radical. When carbon walls are close to each other, the micropores form, resulting in a slit-designed channel. The CDs micropores enhance the adsorption by overlapping the contact potential of both pore walls, resulting in O=O bond deformation. Electrons are required to generate the O2 •− radical. Fortunately, carbons possessing the conductive sp2-conjugated carbon walls have a high concentration of π∗ electrons. The occurrence of doped heteroatoms (N and S atoms) in the carbon dot lattice may increase the carbon's ability to donate electrons to the adsorbed O2.

The CDs acted as a signal molecule, interacting with the receptor proteins on the plant cell membrane surface. A series of steps are activated following the recognition and adherence of the nanomaterial to the plant cell membrane. The mode of action of the nanomaterial as an elicitor varies depending on the concentration, physiochemical environment, size of the nanomaterial, functional groups present on its surface, and charge. The plants could have responded to the CDs by actively exchanging ions, such as Ca2+/H+ influxes and K+/Na+/Cl− effluxes, into the cytosol's plasma membrane. Ca2+ influx has been linked to several cellular and physiological pathways (Ghosh et al., 2022), and it has an important role during the initial stages of the elicitation mechanism. Similarly, cGMP-dependent protein kinase is activated by the oxidative burst in plant cells. Furthermore, these activated protein kinases phosphorylate mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPKs), which modulate gene expression. Most of these transcriptional reprogramming activities may stimulate the pathways that produce and enhance the accumulation of secondary metabolites in plant cells. The receptors on the plasma membrane's surface recognize and bind to the elicitor, triggering a series of reaction events (Fig. 14 ), including G-protein activation, cytoplasmic acidification, ion fluxes, Ca2+ burst, ROS burst, MAPK phosphorylation, and NADPH oxidase activation (Zhao et al., 2005). Ca2+ signalling causes conformational shifts in various Ca2+−binding proteins, especially calmodulin, secondary messengers such as diacylglycerol (DAG), calcium-dependent kinases (CDPKs), phospholipases, calmodulin-related proteins, and inositol 1,4,5-triphosphate IP3 (Patra et al., 2021). Calmodulin/Ca2+-mediated pathways exhibited stimulatory effects on physiological and biochemical responses and cellular functions of the hairy roots, such as hormone signalling, oxidative burst, protein phosphorylation, and gene expression (Lala, 2021). The production of ROS by oxidases such as NADPH oxidase and Ca2+ is equally significant in the plant defence response (Marcec et al., 2019). Calcium flux is primarily the first event that is triggered during the interactions between elicitor and plant cell (Ma et al., 2013), while NADPH oxidase and other corresponding oxidases, which are sometimes activated by asymmetrical Ca2+ movements, also contribute to ROS creation in plant cells (Lala, 2021). G-proteins are also involved in the activation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), phospholipase C, ion channels, phospholipase A, and apoptosis of the cells (Pandey et al., 2015). The de novo production of stress signalling molecules may additionally indirectly stimulate the production and accumulation of secondary metabolites. This facilitates the plant defence response by transforming various metabolic pathways. Activated G-proteins boost the amounts of IP3, cAMP, and DAG, which in turn activates the targeted PKC and PKA. These proteins cause MAPKs to get phosphorylated, which in turn causes transcription and translation of the gene, which triggers enzymatic processes and rewires the pathway responsible for producing secondary metabolites (Lala, 2021).

Agrobacterium rhizogenes typically delivers T-DNA pieces from the Ri plasmid (root-inducing) to the genome of the host plant. The transfer of these genes results in the induction and proliferation of hairy roots at the point of infection in plants. Hairy root production in plant cells infected with A. rhizogenes is often regimented by the rol genes (A, B, or C) (Nilsson and Olsson, 1997). Several studies have shown that distinct rol genes stimulate hairy root formation and improve secondary metabolism, with rolA, rolB, and rolC receiving the most attention (Zhou et al., 2011). In the present research findings, PCR amplifications of the rolA genes using MTCC 532 and hairy roots genomic DNA provided the appropriate amplicon size, demonstrating their expression in radish (Fig. 15 ).

The retention factors for stigmasterol and squalene were determined to be 0.36 and 0.89, respectively. Fig. 16 illustrates the findings of the fingerprints of stigmasterol and squalene in E. alsinoides. The calibration curve was linear in the 0.5–3 ng range, and the correlation coefficients (r) for stigmasterol and squalene indicated a strong linear reliance of peak area on concentration. The linear equations for stigmasterol y = 1332.6x + 199.98 (R2 = 0.9823) (Fig. 17 A) while squalene y = 1.18x + 207.13 (R2 = 0.97549) (Fig. 17B) were used to depict the calibration curve (in which the x value of concentration and the y response are the peak areas). Our findings show that 6 μg mL−1 of the carbon dot elicited hairy root contained 1113.052 ng and 1435.2 ng of stigmasterol and squalene, respectively. The hairy roots had the highest accumulation of squalene compared to the control plant from the wild (Table 3 ). An effective correlation between the standard and sample was detected as well. HPTLC is the preferred approach due to its ease of preparing the sample and ability to analyze several herbal product samples concurrently in a short period of time. There has been no report of simultaneous assessment of stigmasterol and squalene, which may be used to differentiate E. alsinoides preparations. As a result, we created a simple and accurate approach for the simultaneous quantification of squalene and stigmasterol. The present research findings reported a high squalene accumulation in E. alsinoides than that reported by Awere et al. (2024) who also quantified squalene from the elicitation of in vitro micropropagated E. alsinoides. Similarly, the amount of stigmasterol detected in the hairy roots of E. alsinoides was higher than that reported by Sethiya and Mishra (2015) who also simultaneously quantified stigmasterol, ursolic acid, betulin and and lupeol using HPTLC.

The addition of 6 μg mL−1 of carbon dot into the liquid MS medium enhanced the production of valuable metabolites. Cellular protective systems played an important role in stress conditions by increasing antioxidant enzyme activity. This work established an effective protocol for the hairy root transformation of E. alsinoides and a reliable and quick HPTLC dual quantification of SM. Metabolic stress response of E. alsinoides was predicted and optimized by utilizing machine learning. Furthermore, the modification of ML demonstrated a forward-thinking approach to optimize and predict in vitro culture systems. This protocol provides significant information regarding the usage of carbon dots as a signal molecule as well as the biochemical responses of exposed-hairy roots.
